---
title: "Warm-up: determinants"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

- Compute the determinant of a $2\times2$ matrix using one short formula.
- Compute the determinant of a $3\times3$ matrix by cofactor expansion.
- Recognise when a determinant is $0$ and know what that means in one sentence.

## The rule

For a $2\times2$ matrix the determinant is **one number**, found by:
$$
\det\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} = ad - bc.
$$

Multiply the two numbers on the **main diagonal** ($a$ and $d$), then subtract
the product of the other two ($b$ and $c$). That is it.

**Tiny worked example.**
$$
\det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
= (2)(4) - (1)(3) = 8 - 3 = 5.
$$

For a $3\times3$ matrix we use **cofactor expansion along the first row**:
$$
\det\begin{bmatrix} a & b & c \\ d & e & f \\ g & h & i \end{bmatrix}
= a\det\begin{bmatrix} e & f \\ h & i \end{bmatrix}
- b\det\begin{bmatrix} d & f \\ g & i \end{bmatrix}
+ c\det\begin{bmatrix} d & e \\ g & h \end{bmatrix}.
$$

Note the signs: **plus, minus, plus**. Each small determinant is just the
$2\times2$ rule again, on the matrix you get by **crossing out** the row and
column of the front number.

**What $\det = 0$ means.** If $\det(A) = 0$ the matrix is called *singular*: a
system $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ then has **no unique solution**. If
$\det(A) \neq 0$ there is exactly one solution. For now just compute the
number; the meaning comes later.

## Drills

Work these on paper. Start at the top — the first ones are almost trivial.

**2×2 determinants**

1. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
2. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$
3. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$
4. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$
5. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$
6. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$
7. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$
8. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 6 & 5 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$
9. $\det\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
10. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ -1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$

**Is the determinant zero?**

11. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$ — is it $0$?
12. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 6 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ — is it $0$?
13. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$ — is it $0$?

**3×3 determinants (cofactor expansion)**

14. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
15. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$
16. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$
17. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$
18. $\det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$

## Check in R

In R the function is [`det`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/det.html). You build a
matrix with [`matrix`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matrix.html), filling **by
column** (`byrow = FALSE` is the default), or pass `byrow = TRUE` to fill by
row as written on paper.

```r
A <- matrix(c(2, 1,
              3, 4), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
det(A)
# [1] 5      (matches drill-style example)

B <- matrix(c(1, 2,
              2, 4), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
det(B)
# [1] 0      (singular: rows are multiples of each other)

C <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3,
              0, 1, 4,
              0, 0, 2), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
det(C)
# [1] 2      (triangular: product of the diagonal)
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — the 2×2 formula"}
Diagonal product minus anti-diagonal product: $ad - bc$. Write the four numbers
down first, then plug in.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — a zero in the matrix"}
If a number is $0$, the term it multiplies is $0$ and disappears. For example
$\det\begin{bmatrix} a & 0 \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} = ad - 0 = ad$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — checking for zero determinant"}
Compute $ad - bc$ as usual. If one row is just a multiple of the other (e.g.
the second row is $2\times$ the first), the result will be $0$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 4 — 3×3 sign pattern"}
Expanding along the top row, the signs are $+,\,-,\,+$. For each front number,
cross out its row and column and take the $2\times2$ determinant of what is
left.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 5 — triangular shortcut"}
If everything below (or above) the diagonal is $0$, the determinant is just the
product of the diagonal entries. Use this to check drills 14–17.
:::

## Step-by-step solutions

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solutions"}

**Drill 1.** $\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = (1)(1) - (0)(0) = 1 - 0 = 1.$

**Drill 2.** $(2)(3) - (0)(0) = 6 - 0 = 6.$

**Drill 3.** $(5)(4) - (0)(0) = 20 - 0 = 20.$

**Drill 4.** $(1)(3) - (2)(0) = 3 - 0 = 3.$

**Drill 5.** $(2)(2) - (1)(1) = 4 - 1 = 3.$

**Drill 6.** $(3)(4) - (2)(1) = 12 - 2 = 10.$

**Drill 7.** $(4)(3) - (1)(2) = 12 - 2 = 10.$

**Drill 8.** $(6)(2) - (5)(2) = 12 - 10 = 2.$

**Drill 9.** $(-1)(1) - (2)(3) = -1 - 6 = -7.$

**Drill 10.** $(2)(4) - (-3)(-1) = 8 - 3 = 5.$

**Drill 11.** $(1)(4) - (2)(2) = 4 - 4 = 0.$ **Yes**, the determinant is $0$
(the second row is $2\times$ the first).

**Drill 12.** $(3)(2) - (1)(6) = 6 - 6 = 0.$ **Yes**, it is $0$ (second row is
$2\times$ the first).

**Drill 13.** $(2)(3) - (1)(4) = 6 - 4 = 2.$ **No**, it is $2 \neq 0$.

**Drill 14.** Expand along the top row:
$$
1\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
- 0\det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
+ 0\det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
= 1(1) - 0 + 0 = 1.
$$

**Drill 15.** Top row is $2, 0, 0$:
$$
2\det\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} - 0 + 0
= 2(12 - 0) = 2(12) = 24.
$$
(Diagonal shortcut: $2\cdot3\cdot4 = 24$.)

**Drill 16.** Top row $1, 2, 0$:
$$
1\det\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
- 2\det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
+ 0.
$$
First minor: $(3)(2)-(1)(0) = 6$. Second minor: $(0)(2)-(1)(0) = 0$.
So $1(6) - 2(0) + 0 = 6$. (Diagonal shortcut: $1\cdot3\cdot2 = 6$.)

**Drill 17.** Top row $1, 2, 3$:
$$
1\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
- 2\det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
+ 3\det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Minors: $(1)(2)-(4)(0) = 2$; $(0)(2)-(4)(0) = 0$; $(0)(0)-(1)(0) = 0$.
So $1(2) - 2(0) + 3(0) = 2$. (Diagonal shortcut: $1\cdot1\cdot2 = 2$.)

**Drill 18.** Top row $2, 1, 0$:
$$
2\det\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
- 1\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
+ 0.
$$
First minor: $(3)(2)-(1)(1) = 6 - 1 = 5$. Second minor: $(1)(2)-(1)(0) = 2$.
So $2(5) - 1(2) + 0 = 10 - 2 = 8$.

:::
